ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for tutu kueh; mapped to closest parent class 'steamed rice cake' (FOODON_03315595). Tutu kueh is a specific Singaporean steamed rice flour kueh with coconut or peanut filling.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (rice-based snack) is the closest defensible match. No dedicated tutu kueh entry exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for tutu kueh or equivalent steamed rice kueh category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of tutu kueh is one piece, weighing approximately 30 grams, providing around 94 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Tutu Kueh — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Tutu Kueh (F11090064). All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this canonical Singapore source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — steamed rice-based snack analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for tutu kueh exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated by analogy to steamed white rice flour-based snacks and rice cakes (GI range 60–72 in University of Sydney GI Database). The medium GI classification is consistent with the refined rice flour base and moderate sugar content (10.75g/100g). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 15.34g) / 100 = 9.97 per 30g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (10.75g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.75g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention despite very low sodium (7mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Sodium is very low at 7mg/100g, but the high sugar and low fibre profile promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention; individuals with hypertension should limit to 1–2 pieces per occasion and avoid pairing with other high-sugar foods.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate (51.13g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.75g/100g) and medium GI (65) produces a significant glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tutu kueh's high refined carbohydrate load (51.13g/100g) and low fibre (1.75g/100g) result in rapid postprandial glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should treat this as an occasional indulgence, limit to 1 piece, and pair with a protein source to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut filling (~5.8g/100g) and high sugar content (10.75g/100g) contribute to atherogenic and metabolic risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The coconut-derived saturated fat (~5.8g/100g) and high sugar content (10.75g/100g) in tutu kueh may adversely affect LDL cholesterol and triglyceride levels; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional small portions and prioritise lower-saturated-fat snack alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Coconut-derived saturated fat (~5.8g/100g) and high-GI refined rice flour base promote pro-inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The saturated fat from desiccated coconut and the high-GI refined rice flour base may promote systemic inflammation; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit tutu kueh to occasional consumption and favour anti-inflammatory snack alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tutu kueh (also spelled tutu kuih) is a traditional Singaporean steamed snack made from finely milled rice flour pressed into a small, round bamboo mould and steamed until set. The kueh is typically filled with either sweetened shredded coconut (desiccated coconut mixed with palm sugar) or ground roasted peanuts mixed with sugar. The outer shell is soft, slightly chewy, and delicately flavoured, while the filling provides a sweet, aromatic contrast. Tutu kueh is a beloved street food and hawker snack in Singapore, commonly sold by vendors who steam the individual pieces to order in traditional bamboo steamers. It is associated with Malay and Peranakan culinary heritage and is popular as a breakfast snack or teatime treat. Nutritionally, tutu kueh is carbohydrate-dominant (51.13g/100g) with moderate fat (7.0g/100g, predominantly saturated from coconut), modest protein (5.84g/100g), and low sodium (7mg/100g). The sugar content is notable at 10.75g/100g from palm sugar, and dietary fibre is low at 1.75g/100g. The medium glycemic index (~65) and high carbohydrate load make it unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. It is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, vegan, and halal. One piece weighs approximately 30g and provides around 94 kcal.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F11090064), which is the canonical primary source for Singaporean foods. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using steamed rice flour-based snack equivalents, which is the approved GI source. Confidence is high for macros and secondary nutrients; medium for GI estimate.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for tutu kueh are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F11090064) as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat value of 5.8g/100g is estimated from the coconut filling composition (desiccated coconut is approximately 57–60% saturated fat by weight) and is consistent with the total fat of 7.0g/100g; this estimate is documented for transparency. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on steamed rice flour-based snack equivalents from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for tutu kueh was found. Glycemic load per 30g serving = (65 × 15.34) / 100 = 9.97. All core macros (protein_g 5.84, fat_g 7.0, carbohydrate_g 51.13) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification required.
